---
title: "Supplemental"
author: "Holly K. Nesbitt"
date: "September 27, 2024"
output: 
    html_document:
        toc: true
        toc_float: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE} 
knitr::opts_chunk$set(warning = FALSE, message = FALSE) 
```

Note: Much of this is probably unnecessary for the published supplemental material. Because I'm so new to Bayesian analysis, I'm including it here for co-authors to:  
  
* see if there's anything critical that I've omitted,  
* get feedback on what should be included in a supplemental file, and  
* identify if there are issues with my modelling approach that should be tweaked.
    + Some issues with heteroskedasticity still - see Diagnostics/Residual plots (setting priors for NRD SD's didn't appear to make a difference)
    + Still some large differences in informative vs non-informative priors - see Model checks/Is there a notable effect of the prior when compared with non-informative priors? (I set flat priors, but those seem to be more informative to me since they allow for equal probability of larger numbers in a logistic. I also tried narrower normal distributions but then those tend to pull the estimates toward 0... everything seems mildly informative to me)  
    
I'll wind up hiding most of the code here but I'm including it for now so you can see what I did...

# Survey details
Individuals unaffiliated with the research group piloted the questionnaire.  
We purchased producer address information from Farm Market iD, an agricultural data marketing company, requesting addresses within our study counties for all National Agricultural Survey respondents with >20 acres of pasture/rangeland (N = 6546 with mailing addresses, 3448 for whom we had emails). After taking a simple random sample of 4500 from this population and removing duplicates, our sample size was 4494 individuals, 2409 for whom we had emails. Using a modified tailored design (Dillman, Smyth, and Christian 2014), we sent three survey invitation emails followed by three paper mailings; all mailings were spaced 2-3 weeks apart. 

```{r, , include = FALSE}
library(brms)
library(rstan)
library(tidyverse)
library(splines)
library(MASS)
```

# Data prep
```{r}
# read in data
dat90_20 <- 
    read_csv("data/processed/ProducerDF_TreeCoverChangeCounty_nrd.csv") 

# remove NAs
dat90_20_nas <- 
    na.omit(dat90_20 %>%
                dplyr::select(uniqueID, trees1_9020, trees100_9020, b_burn01, b_remo01, group_involve2, nrd)) #leaves 396 observations

# rescale - divide continuous variables by 2 sds instead of 1 so that they are comparable on the same scale (see Gelman)
dat90_20_z<-dat90_20_nas %>% 
    mutate(trees1_9020 = arm::rescale(trees1_9020),
           trees100_9020 = arm::rescale(trees100_9020))

```

# Model specification
## Priors
We expected that larger numbers are less likely for the parameter estimate for all of our variables, so we used a normal distribution centered at 0 with a standard deviation (sd) of 1.  
We expected a narrow distribution of estimates for the intercept, so we used a student-t distribution with degrees of freedom (df) of 7, median (mu) of 0, and a scale parameter (sigma) of 1.5.  
The standard deviation must be above 0, so we used an exponential distribution with a rate of 1 which truncates estimates above 0.

```{r}
prior1 <- c(set_prior("normal(0,1)", class = "b", coef = "group_involve2"), #expect that larger numbers would be less likely
            set_prior("normal(0,1)", class = "b", coef = "trees1_9020"),
            set_prior("normal(0,1)", class = "b", coef = "trees100_9020"),
            set_prior("normal(0,1)", class = "b", coef = "trees100_9020:trees1_9020"),
            set_prior("student_t(7, 0, 1.5)", class = "b", coef = "Intercept"), #expect a narrow distribution
            set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd"), #truncates above 0
            set_prior("exponential(0.5)", class = "sd", coef = "Intercept", group = "nrd")) #truncates above zero, allows SD to be wider for NRD effects

```

## Model controls
We had a warmup period of 1000 with 3000 iterations. We used 2 cores and 2 chains. We used the Bernoulli family (with the logit link) to model a logistic regression. 
```{r}
output <- capture.output(burn <- brm(b_burn01 ~
                 0 + Intercept +
                 trees100_9020 + 
                 trees1_9020 +
                 trees100_9020*trees1_9020 +
                 group_involve2 +
                 (1|nrd),
             data = dat90_20_z, 
             warmup = 1000, iter = 3000, 
             cores = 2, chains = 4, 
             control = list(adapt_delta = 0.96),
             prior = prior1,
             seed = 123, 
             family = bernoulli,
             sample_prior = T))
```

# Results
## Standardized results
```{r}
knitr::kable(fixef(burn), caption = "Table S1: Posterior estimates (standardized) for the fixed effects in the model. These values correspond to the posterior estimates shown in Figure 5 of the main body of the paper.")
```

![Figure S1: The effect of Natural Resources Districts (NRDs) on prescribed burning. NRDs are plotted in order from the northwest corner of the state (Upper Niobrara-White) to the southwest corner of the state (Nemaha) following the juniper encroachment gradient. NRDs with juniper cost share programs have a green density distribution and NRDs that have eliminated juniper cost share programs have a grey density distribution. The point is the median, the thick bars cover the middle 66% of the density distribution, and the thin bars cover the middle 95% of the density distribution.](/Users/hollynesbitt/Analyses/PHDScalarMismatches/figs/nrd_effect.png)


# Plot of the predictions vs observations (standardized)

![Figure S2: Predicted probability of prescribed burning, holding regional-level mean % tree cover constant at the average. Lines are the median posterior probability, bands are the 95% credibility intervals, and jittered points are the observed binary data (0/1).](/Users/hollynesbitt/Analyses/PHDScalarMismatches/figs/logistic1_bayesian_burn.png)

![Figure S3: Predicted probability of prescribed burning, holding local-level mean % tree cover constant at the average. Lines are the median posterior probability, bands are the 95% credibility intervals, and jittered points are the observed binary data (0/1).](/Users/hollynesbitt/Analyses/PHDScalarMismatches/figs/logistic100_bayesian_burn.png)

# Diagnostics
## Posterior predictive check
Generated data matches real data quite closely - indicates model is correctly specified (partly assessed w posterior predictive checks).
```{r}
pp_check(burn, ndraws = 100) 
```

## Residual plots
trees1_9020 - bands due to 0/1s, more error variation at lower values of x. This is partly because most values for trees1_9020 (see fig in plot showing tree cover over time) are near 0 but there are quite a few large values. I've tried adding an x2 variable and it doesn't fix the problem. I'm not sure how much it matters? It's clearly a terrible explanatory variable but needs to be in the model for the interaction effect.  
```{r, include = FALSE}
pp_check(burn, type = "error_scatter_avg_vs_x", size = 1.1, 
         x = "trees1_9020")
```

trees100_9020 - bands due to 0/1s. As tree cover gets more severe at the regional level, the model gets both better (top band going to 0) and worse (bottom band going to -1) in equal measure (probably getting better at predicting 1s and worse at predicting 0s). I'm not sure what to do about this or how important it is. I think I'd prefer to caveat this rather than fight forever to get this pattern to disappear.
```{r, include = FALSE}
pp_check(burn, type = "error_scatter_avg_vs_x", size = 1.1, 
         x = "trees100_9020")
```
interaction - a few wild cards for large values of x, maybe a bit of patterning but not too bad.
```{r, include = FALSE}
# To look at the residuals for the interaction effect, I frist tried the commented out code but it didn't work. So I generated a new column (dat90_20_z$trees100_9020* dat90_20_z$trees1_9020) and ran a new model treating it as a separate variable instead of an interaction effect. There's probably a better way to do this though. 

# dat90_20_z$`trees100_9020:trees1_9020` <- interaction(dat90_20_z$trees100_9020, dat90_20_z$trees1_9020)
# pp_check(burn, type = "error_scatter_avg_vs_x", size = 1.1,
#          x = "trees100_9020:trees1_9020")


dat90_20_z$interaction <- dat90_20_z$trees100_9020*dat90_20_z$trees1_9020

prior_int_col <- c(set_prior("normal(0,1)", class = "b", coef = "group_involve2"), # expect that larger numbers would be less likely
            set_prior("normal(0,1)", class = "b", coef = "trees1_9020"),
            set_prior("normal(0,1)", class = "b", coef = "trees100_9020"),
            set_prior("normal(0,1)", class = "b", coef = "interaction"),
            set_prior("student_t(7, 0, 1.5)", class = "b", coef = "Intercept"), # expect a narrow distribution
            set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd"), #truncates above 0
            set_prior("exponential(0.5)", class = "sd", coef = "Intercept", group = "nrd")) #truncates above zero, allows SD to be wider for NRD effect

output <- capture.output(burn_int_col <- brm(b_burn01 ~
                 0 + Intercept +
                 trees100_9020 + 
                 trees1_9020 +
                 interaction +
                 group_involve2 +
                 (1|nrd),
             data = dat90_20_z, 
             warmup = 1000, iter = 3000, 
             cores = 2, chains = 2, 
             control = list(adapt_delta = 0.96),
             prior = prior_int_col,
             seed = 123, 
             family = bernoulli,
             sample_prior = T))

pp_check(burn_int_col, type = "error_scatter_avg_vs_x", size = 1.1,
         x = "interaction")
```

## Multicollinearity
No problems.
```{r}
pairs(burn, pars = "b", 
      off_diag_args =  
          list(size = 0.5,
               alpha = 0.25))[1:5,1:5]
```

# Model checks
## Chain convergence
Chains converge.  
Rhats are all 1.  
Shrinks to 1, so used enough iterations.  
Geweke diagnostic not greater than 1.96 in last iteration in chain.  

```{r}
plot(burn)
summary(burn)
coda::gelman.plot(as.mcmc(burn)[, 1:5])
coda::geweke.diag(as.mcmc(burn)[, 1:5])
coda::geweke.plot(as.mcmc(burn)[, 1:5])
```

### Chain convergence after doubling the number of iterations
Yes, relative bias is less than 5%.

```{r}
output <- capture.output(burn_iter2 <- brm(b_burn01 ~
                       0 + Intercept +
                       trees100_9020 + 
                       trees1_9020 +
                       trees100_9020*trees1_9020 +
                       group_involve2 +
                       (1|nrd),
                   data = dat90_20_z, 
                   warmup = 1000, iter = 6000, 
                   cores = 2, chains = 4, 
                   control = list(adapt_delta = 0.96),
                   prior = prior1,
                   seed = 123, 
                   family = bernoulli,
                   sample_prior = T)) #saves priors for plots later

coda::gelman.diag(as.mcmc(burn_iter2)[, 1:5])
coda::gelman.plot(as.mcmc(burn_iter2)[, 1:5])

round(100*((summary(burn_iter2)$fixed - summary(burn)$fixed) / summary(burn)$fixed), 3)[,"Estimate"] #relative bias is <5%
```

## Autocorrelation
trees_100_9020 has some autocorrelation still after a few lags but eventually dissipates.
```{r}
bayesplot::mcmc_acf(burn)
coda::autocorr.diag(as.mcmc(burn)[,1:5], lags = c(0, 1,2,3,4, 5, 10, 50))
```

## Does the posterior distribution histogram have enough information?
Yes, peaked with smooth slopes.
```{r}
mcmc_plot(burn, type = "hist")
mcmc_plot(burn_iter2, type = "hist")
```
## Effect of priors
```{r}
priors <- prior_draws(burn) %>%
    mutate(obs = (c(1:8000))) %>% 
    pivot_longer(!obs, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")

posteriors <- as.data.frame(burn) %>%
    dplyr::select(b_Intercept:sd_nrd__Intercept) %>%
    mutate(obs = (c(1:8000))) %>% 
    pivot_longer(!obs, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")
    
ggplot() +
    geom_density(data = priors, aes(x = value)) +
    geom_density(data = posteriors, aes(x = value), color = "blue") +
    facet_wrap(~ variable) +
    labs(title = "Prior Distribution vs. Posterior Distribution") +
    xlim(-5, 5)
```



## Is there a notable effect of the prior when compared with non-informative priors?
Between 13 and 33% changes in effect size (except for trees1 - the percent change in the effect was large but the estimate is still very close to 0)
```{r}
prior_uninf <- c(set_prior("uniform(-2,2)", class = "b", coef = "group_involve2"),
            set_prior("uniform(-3,3)", class = "b", coef = "trees1_9020"),
            set_prior("uniform(-3,3)", class = "b", coef = "trees100_9020"),
            set_prior("uniform(-4,4)", class = "b", coef = "trees100_9020:trees1_9020"),
            set_prior("uniform(-5,5)", class = "b", coef = "Intercept"),
            set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd"),
            set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd", coef = "Intercept", group = "nrd"))

# prior_uninf <- c(set_prior("uniform(-2,2)", class = "b", coef = "group_involve2"), 
#             set_prior("normal(0, 2)", class = "b", coef = "trees1_9020"),
#             set_prior("normal(0, 2)", class = "b", coef = "trees100_9020"),
#             set_prior("normal(0, 2)", class = "b", coef = "trees100_9020:trees1_9020"),
#             set_prior("normal(0, 2)", class = "b", coef = "Intercept"), 
#             set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd"),
#             set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd", coef = "Intercept", group = "nrd"))



output <- capture.output(burn_uninf <- brm(b_burn01 ~
                       0 + Intercept +
                       trees100_9020 + 
                       trees1_9020 +
                       trees100_9020*trees1_9020 +
                       group_involve2 +
                       (1|nrd),
                   data = dat90_20_z, 
                   warmup = 1000, iter = 3000, 
                   cores = 2, chains = 4, 
                   control = list(adapt_delta = 0.96),
                   prior = prior_uninf,
                   seed = 123, 
                   family = bernoulli,
                   sample_prior = T))

summary(burn)
summary(burn_uninf)

round(100*((summary(burn_uninf)$fixed - summary(burn)$fixed) / summary(burn)$fixed), 3)[,"Estimate"]
```
```{r}
priors_uninf <- prior_draws(burn_uninf) %>%
    mutate(obs = (c(1:8000))) %>% 
    pivot_longer(!obs, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")

posteriors_uninf <- as.data.frame(burn_uninf) %>%
    dplyr::select(b_Intercept:sd_nrd__Intercept) %>%
    mutate(obs = (c(1:8000))) %>% 
    pivot_longer(!obs, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")
    
ggplot() +
    geom_density(data = priors_uninf, aes(x = value)) +
    geom_density(data = posteriors_uninf, aes(x = value), color = "blue") +
    facet_wrap(~ variable) +
    labs(title = "Prior Distribution vs. Posterior Distribution") +
    xlim(-5, 5)
```




# Model fit
## R2
Not amazing, but not terrible.
```{r}
bayes_R2(burn)
```
## WAIC
WAIC is much lower for burn than for the null model.
```{r}
output <- capture.output(burn0 <- update(burn, formula = b_burn01 ~ 1, refresh = 0, newdata = dat90_20_z))
```
```{r}
waic(burn)
waic(burn0)
```
## LOO
burn performs much better than burn0 according to leave-one-out cross-validation.
```{r}
loo0<-loo(burn0)
loo1<-loo(burn, save_psis = T)
loo_compare(loo0, loo1)
```

# Classification error
## Posterior classification accuracy
Provides a generous perspective on model accuracy.
```{r}
#predicted probabilities
linpred <- posterior_linpred(burn)
preds <- posterior_epred(burn)
pred <- colMeans(preds)
pr <- as.integer(pred >= 0.5)

dat90_20_z %>% 
    dplyr::select(b_burn01) %>%
    bind_cols(pr) %>%
    rename(predicted = `...2`) %>%
    mutate(correct = if_else(b_burn01 == predicted, 1, 0)) %>%
    summarize(mean_correct = mean(correct))
```

## LOO classification accuracy
LOO provides a more conservative estimate of classification accuracy.  
But there were only small differences in the posterior predictive probabilities and the LOO probabilities.
```{r}
## LOO predictive probabilities
ploo <- loo::E_loo(preds, loo1$psis_object, type="mean", log_ratios = -log_lik(burn))$value
ploo_01 <- as.integer(ploo >= 0.5)

dat90_20_z %>% 
    dplyr::select(b_burn01) %>%
    bind_cols(ploo_01) %>%
    rename(predicted = `...2`) %>%
    mutate(correct = if_else(b_burn01 == predicted, 1, 0)) %>%
    summarize(mean_correct = mean(correct))

qplot(pred, ploo) 
```

## k-fold cross-validation
k-fold cross-validation provides the most conservative estimate of model accuracy.
```{r}
library(caret)
library(glmmTMB)
library(verification) #for roc.plot()

# Randomly partition data into 10 test folds
set.seed(1234)

dat_folds <-
    dat90_20_z %>% 
    mutate(fold = factor(sample(rep(1:10, length.out=nrow(.))),
                         labels = 1:10)) 

# Get model formula of your model of interest
model_formula <- formula(burn)

# Make a list, where each element is a separate test fold
test_list <- list()
for (i in levels(dat_folds$fold)) {
    test_list[[i]] <- filter(dat_folds, fold == i) 
}

# Do the same thing, but for training folds
train_list <- list()
for (i in levels(dat_folds$fold)) {
    train_list[[i]] <- filter(dat_folds, fold != i) 
}

# Then put the lists into a dataframe, so the first column is the fold number,
# and the next to columns are list-columns
folds <- tibble(fold_group = levels(dat_folds$fold),
                train_data = train_list,
                test_data = test_list)

# Then you can apply your model to each of the 10 training folds
output <- capture.output(folds_models <- folds %>%
    mutate(model = purrr::map(train_data, ~brm(model_formula, data=.)))) 

# And then make predictions from each of those 10 models using the corresponding test data as new data
test_predict <-
    folds_models %>% 
    mutate(predict = purrr::map2(model, test_data, 
                          ~predict(.x, newdata=.y, allow.new.levels=T, re.form=NA, type = "response")))

# Then you can pull out the stuff you want (folds, test data, and predictions) and unnest it (no more list-columns)
test_predict_df <-   
    test_predict %>% 
    dplyr::select(fold_group, test_data, predict) %>% 
    unnest(c(test_data, predict))

# determine appropriate classification threshold
roc.plot(test_predict_df$b_burn01, test_predict_df$predict[,1])

# Classify each prediction that's above a threshold as a 1, and all others as a 0
threshold <- 0.5

# Classify whether or not the predictions are correct
# Then get the proportion that are correct (same as a mean, since they're all binary) for each fold
test_predict_df %>% 
    mutate(pred_01 = if_else(predict[,1] > threshold, 1, 0), # play with threshold
           correct = if_else(b_burn01 == pred_01, 1, 0)) %>% 
    group_by(fold_group) %>% 
    summarize(prop_correct = mean(correct)) %>% 
    summarize(mean = mean(prop_correct),
              sd = sd(prop_correct),
              min = min(prop_correct),
              max = max(prop_correct))
```

## Is the model better at predicting 0s or 1s?
About the same at 0.5 threshold.
```{r}
test_predict_df %>% 
    mutate(pred_01 = if_else(predict[,1] > threshold, 1, 0),
           correct = if_else(b_burn01 == pred_01, 1, 0)) %>% 
    group_by(fold_group) %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = predict[,1], y = correct)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.7, aes(col = as.factor(b_burn01))) +
    annotate("text",label = "true negative\ncorrect, no burn", x = 0.2, y = 0.8) +
    annotate("text", label = "true positive\ncorrect, burn", x = 0.7, y = 0.8) +
    annotate("text", label = "false negative\nincorrect\npredicted no burn, but burned", x = 0.2, y = 0.2) +
    annotate("text", label = "false positive\nincorrect\npredicted burn, but didn't", x = 0.7, y = 0.2) 

test_predict_df %>% 
    mutate(pred_01 = if_else(predict[,1] > threshold, 1, 0)) %>% 
    group_by(b_burn01, pred_01) %>%
    summarize(n = n()) %>%
    mutate(false_true = case_when(b_burn01 == 0 & pred_01 == 0 ~ "true_neg",
                                  b_burn01 == 1 & pred_01 == 1 ~ "true_pos",
                                  b_burn01 == 0 & pred_01 == 1 ~ "false_pos",
                                  b_burn01 == 1 & pred_01 == 0 ~ "false_neg"))

```

## Sensitivity analysis for threshold
Average model accuracy peaks at a threshold of 0.5.  
false negative rate = false positive rate = 0.36 at about 0.44 threshold.  
If you were to actually use this to identify potential burners, you may want to reduce the false negative rate (so lower the threshold) so that you cast a wider net (unless you were resource limited, in which case you might want to reduce the false positive rate).
```{r}
threshold <-seq(0,1,0.05)
fn<-c(NA,NA, 0.006, 0.04, 0.06,0.06,0.1,0.14, 0.27, 0.37,0.42,0.47,0.55,0.63,0.71,0.82,0.88,0.94, NA,NA,NA)
fp<-c(NA,NA, 0.97, 0.80, 0.67,0.65,0.63, 0.58, 0.43, 0.32, 0.26,0.23,0.17,0.13,0.08,0.05,0.01,0.009,NA,NA, NA)
acc_avg<-c(0.46, 0.46, 0.47, 0.55, 0.61,0.61,0.61,0.62, 0.64, 0.65, 0.67,0.66, 0.65, 0.65, 0.64, 0.59,0.59, 0.56, 0.55, 0.55, 0.55)

tibble(threshold, fn, fp, acc_avg) %>%
    pivot_longer(!threshold, 
                 names_to = "stat",
                 values_to = "rate") %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = threshold, y = rate, group = stat)) +
    geom_line(aes(col = stat))
```
